content:
5 min read
Just now

--

No server, no login, no subscription. The constraint sounded absurd — until it started making the software better.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The $480 receipt
Two years into freelancing, I did something I’d been avoiding: I added up what I had paid for invoicing software. A little over $480.
Then I listed what that money had actually bought. A form where I typed line items. Some arithmetic a calculator from 1974 could do. A button that made PDFs. A list of clients that I — not the software — had typed in.
I was paying $20 a month to visit my own data.
The subscription model has a quiet assumption buried in it: that software is a service someone performs for you, forever, like mowing a lawn. But my invoices weren’t a lawn. Nothing needed maintaining. The tool wasn’t doing anything between my visits — it was just holding my records hostage in a nicer shirt.
So I cancelled. The confirmation email felt fantastic for about a day. Then a client asked for an invoice.
What invoicing software actually is
Panic is clarifying. Sitting there with no tool, I stripped the problem to its skeleton: a form. Some arithmetic. A saved list of clients. A PDF generator. Somewhere to keep records.
Read that list again. None of it needs a server. None of it needs my email address, a password, or a company in San Francisco staying solvent. My data was making a round trip to someone else’s computer purely so it could be displayed back to me — with a toll booth on the return leg.
That’s when the absurd idea showed up, the kind you dismiss and then can’t stop thinking about:
What if the entire thing were one HTML file?
Files are a superpower we forgot about
Not an app you install. Not a site you log into. A file — like a spreadsheet or an MP3. Double-click, it opens in your browser, it works.
We used to know this. There’s a generation of business owners who ran everything on one heroic Excel file named INVOICES_FINAL_v7_REAL.xls, and for all our mockery, that file had properties modern SaaS can’t match.
You can copy a file. You can email it to yourself. You can put it in Dropbox, on a USB stick, on a laptop with no internet in a cabin. Nobody can raise its price, sunset it, pivot it into an AI note-taker, or lock you out because a payment failed while you were on holiday.
A file is a possession. A subscription is a permission.
The interesting technical bits
Building it taught me more than any framework project has, precisely because the constraint kept saying no to me. A few things that surprised me.
The browser already ships a database. localStorage gives every page about 5 MB of persistent storage. Sounds tiny — until you do the math. My complete records — every client, invoice, time entry, and expense — serialize to under 100 KB. Twenty years of freelancing fits in a megabyte. The “you need a cloud database” assumption dies quickly when you actually weigh your data.
PDFs were the hard part, twice. My first version screenshotted the invoice with a canvas library and stuffed the image into a PDF. It worked — and produced blurry, five-megabyte invoices where you couldn’t select the text. Rewriting against a proper PDF library (real text, real fonts) produced crisp 8 KB files. Lesson: the difference between “technically works” and “professional” is usually one rewrite deep.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Time zones will humiliate you in the smallest apps. For weeks, any invoice I created before 5:30 AM got yesterday’s date. Why 5:30? Because India is UTC+5:30, and I’d stamped dates with toISOString() — which answers in London time regardless of where you live. Every developer east of Greenwich eventually meets this bug at 1 AM. Mine lived in forty characters of a single file, and it still took a real test session to catch.
Demo data needs an exit plan. I added sample records so first-time users see a working dashboard instead of a graveyard of zeros. The sample data deletes itself the moment you add anything real — which worked perfectly until testing revealed it was also deleting duplicates users made from sample invoices, because the copy inherited a hidden flag. Small software still has deep corners.
What the constraint costs
Honesty section. One file means: no sync between devices (your data lives in each browser; you move it with a JSON export and import), no team accounts, no phone push notifications. If you invoice from three devices an hour, this architecture will genuinely annoy you.
But that’s the thing about constraints — they choose their user. A solo freelancer sending a handful of invoices a month doesn’t have a sync problem. They have a $240-a-year problem.
The 2035 test
Here’s the question I now ask about every tool I depend on: will this still open in ten years?
Ask it about your current stack. The SaaS dashboard? It’ll open exactly as long as the company exists and you keep paying. The Electron app? Until the maintainer moves on and the next OS update breaks it.
An HTML file? Browsers have rendered twenty-five-year-old HTML without complaint since before some of my clients were born. Backwards compatibility is the web’s most sacred promise. The file outlives the company — including mine.
You don’t really own software you can’t copy.
Where it ended up
The tool got a name — ZIRO — and after friends kept asking for it, it became the thing I now sell: $29, once, the file is yours forever. In the spirit of everything above, the full app runs as a free live demo in your browser — no signup, it’s the same file — so you can judge it in two minutes.
Demo: https://ziro-landing.vercel.app/try.html
The file (and whatever I build next): https://ziro-landing.vercel.app
And if you’d rather build your own — genuinely, do it. The recipe is up there: one file, localStorage, a PDF library, and a refusal to add anything that needs a server. The point was never my product. The point is that owning your tools is still possible, and it costs a weekend.
I built and sell ZIRO — everything here is my own experience, bugs included.
